verbatim_text: Okay, I guess we'll get started. So I'm very pleased to have with us Amrith Setlur from the Schumann department at CMU. He's giving us today to talk about reinforcement learning, reinforcement learning, and training models to adapt at test time. Thank you, Chris, and thank you all for signing in. Today I'll talk about training models to adapt at test time. Over the past few years, we have seen some amazing progress in AI. We have chatbots, coding agents, and so on. At the same time, there's a long way to go in some other application areas like drug discovery, or proving hard theorems in research math, or even in areas like URS. Now on the left are applications with tons of internet data to train on, but the ones on the right are sort of largely in. So in a nutshell, we are yet to see AI systems that generalize very broadly. Now to see where we can improve, let's take an abstract view of machine learning. So typically we are given this data set of input-output pairs. What we do next is we fit that data, and what we get in the end is a trained static model. Think about a neural network.
